segments.text: Hi everyone. I'd like to introduce
Chanel. Chanel is our developer advocate
here at Google Workspace. And you'll
notice that she's the one behind a lot
of the content that we put on our great
YouTube channel. I'm sure many of you
had had the pleasure of meeting Slash
Chappelle, outbound product manager at
Google Workspace. I highly recommend his
quarterly Google Workspace Road sessions
and I'm happy to be sharing the session
with him today. So today we're going
exploring. We're going on a journey. The
cloud platform that we've used to build
Google Workspace serves as our guide
today. Since your data is safely in our
cloud, you can build tools, manage
tools, and integrate other platforms
that can get a lot of work done for you
without you even having to be at your
computer. In fact, you can do this
without deploying, updating, or
installing any software to your users
thanks to the cloud. This journey is
about adapting Google Workspace to your
needs.
So when I speak to our customers, I get
feedback that it can be a bit
overwhelming, scary or sometimes feel
too complex to do these integrations and
I can really understand why as many of
them have been at the other platform for
decades. So today I present you this
wonderful map which I created with the
help of my incredibly talented design
assistant Gemini.
This map will guide you to the solution
best fitted for your needs. Let's dig
into it. Let's start with the first stop
on our journey, the Google Workspace
marketplace. How does this help
you? Did you know that Workspace has a
marketplace with thousands of apps and
add-ons? This means without having to
create a solution yourself, you can
simply install one created by other
developers.
Customers I work with often have
questions about these apps. Are they
vetted by Google? Can I trust them with
my data? Yes. Before an add-on or an app
is published to the marketplace, it
undergoes two separate reviews by teams
at Google. Oath verification focuses on
making sure that an app doesn't misuse
user data. And the app review focuses on
the information that the app's developer
provides in the Google Workspace
marketplace as well as the functionality
and usability of the app. As a
developer, you can publish Google
Workspace add-ons, editor add-ons,
Google Chat apps, classroom add-ons,
drive apps, and web apps to the Google
Workspace marketplace.
When publishing your solution to the
marketplace, you can decide to make it
available to your organization only or
for anyone with a Google account. And
one of these powerful integrations is
between Gmail, Gemini, and Salesforce.
Yes, that's an add-on that just recently
launched and it brings your Salesforce
data into Gmail with a super handy
helper, Gemini. A great way to deploy a
Google Workspace add-on is as an admin.
You can install it for specific groups
in your organization or your entire
domain. Just as we mentioned, the admin
will at this point be able to assess the
add-on, check what type of data is
accessed, and then install it. So,
there's an extra layer of governance
which many of our customers like having.
Admins can choose to have full control
by disabling users to install add-ons
themselves and instead they can allow
list add-ons as they see fit.
Great. Now I have the ability to run
kind of my own workspace app store with
some of my own apps and some that I have
approved specifically for my
organization.
But I often come across empowered teams
that want to improve their workflows
without involving the IT team. Where
does this land on our map? That's where
App Sheet comes in handy. With App
Sheet, users can create applications
without having to write a single line of
code.
These apps work on desktop and mobile
devices which makes it a great solution
platform for any team including
frontline
workers. Even though it's great to
empower the end user to create their own
solutions, IT departments and admins
need to be able to be aware of what is
being created. That's why the governance
and policy controls for app sheet are so
valuable. So long gone are the days
where macros were shared over email. And
when the author of this macro left the
company, company chaos
ensued. You are in control of what apps
get published and how they are used and
how they are updated.
We have over 50 connectors to third
party systems and you can also use the
app sheet rest API to integrate with any
automations you have going. Okay, so
this seems to be a great enabler for
citizen developers. But in my 30 years
in tech, no code solutions have always
meant some code. How easy is it really?
You can jump start your app creation by
using one of the many templates or
nowadays you only have to tell Gemini
what your app should do and it will
create the app for you. So it's really
no code.
So, we went from a marketplace managed
by admins and checked by Google to a
prompt code solution that empowers teams
to solve their own
challenges. But what about the real
killer, repetitive tasks and my favorite
thing of all time, spreadsheets?
I have just the right capability for
you, macros.
Have a look at this example of how you
can record a macro in Google Sheets to
automate the creation of a pivot
table. As you can see, you record, you
save, and then you run the macro like
magic. The pivot table
appears. When you record macros in
Google Sheets, an appcript function is
automatically created that replicates
the macro steps.
To see the code, select extensions
appcript from the sheets menu. If you
want to, you can change the macro by
editing the code in the appcript
editor. In fact, Google Sheets has not

connect with other systems such as
Oracle, Looker, and Big
Query. But here's a tip I've discovered

with you Sesh in mind. I know that you
often get asked to meet with customers
and I set out on creating a simple
solution to automate this
process. Have requesters fill out the
Google form with information about the
customer date for the meeting and topic.
You then check the submissions and

asks you to meet with a

automatically as well as emails are sent

Google forms touches on sheets calendar
and Gmail. Google Appcript is a rapid
application development platform built
into Google Workspace. It makes
automating a task as we saw the demo
super easy. The first step is to get the
data that was written to Google Sheets
when the Google form was submitted.
Using Appcript's spreadsheet service, we
get the active sheet. Use the handy get
data range method to find the range that
contains data and use the get values
method to access the
data. After that, we can leverage
AppScript's calendar service to create

service to send out email
messages. If you're new to AppScript,
Gemini is a great assistant when you're
creating such automations.
This is actually now part of my workflow
when
coding. Before I write a single line of

idea is feasible. What are my options?
Service account or API key? And to go
beyond everything we've just mentioned,
your dev teams can tap into Workspace

We are using Appcript to create a Google

And that brings us to the end of our
